title: Magnets and magnetic fields — invisible forces that shape our world
examTip: Know the difference between permanent and induced magnets, and be able to describe field line patterns with direction from N to S outside the magnet.
introTips: Magnetic fields are regions of force — not just around magnets but also around current-carrying wires.
Iron is magnetically 'soft' — easy to magnetise and demagnetise. Steel is 'hard' — retains magnetism.
Earth itself acts like a giant bar magnet — this is why compasses work.
intro: Every time you stick something to a fridge, use a compass, or get an MRI scan, you're relying on magnetism. Magnetic fields are invisible, but their effects are real and powerful — and they're also the foundation of how electricity generators and electric motors work. Understand magnets first, and the rest of electromagnetism makes sense.

keyPoints.heading: Permanent and induced magnets
keyPoints.content: A permanent magnet is a material that is always magnetic — it does not need to be placed in a field to exhibit magnetism. Examples include iron, cobalt, nickel, and neodymium alloys. Every permanent magnet has a north pole and a south pole. The fundamental rule is: unlike poles attract (N attracts S) and like poles repel (N repels N, S repels S). The force between magnets acts without physical contact — it is a non-contact force that acts through the magnetic field. An induced (temporary) magnet is a magnetic material that becomes magnetised when placed inside a magnetic field. Remove it from the field and it loses its magnetism. Iron is described as magnetically 'soft' — it is easy to magnetise and easy to demagnetise, making it ideal for induced magnets and electromagnet cores. Steel is magnetically 'hard' — harder to magnetise but retains its magnetism, making it better for permanent magnets. Ferromagnetic materials are those that can be strongly magnetised: iron, steel, cobalt, and nickel. All other materials — aluminium, copper, wood, plastic — are non-magnetic and are unaffected by magnetic fields.
writeBullets: Permanent magnets — always magnetic; examples: iron, cobalt, nickel, neodymium
Unlike poles attract; like poles repel — non-contact force through the field
Induced magnet — magnetised by a field, loses magnetism when removed
Iron is 'soft' (easy to magnetise/demagnetise); steel is 'hard' (retains magnetism)
Ferromagnetic materials — iron, steel, cobalt, nickel — strongly affected by fields
Non-magnetic — aluminium, copper, wood, plastic — unaffected by magnetic fields

keyTerms.def: A material that is always magnetic without needing an external field
A magnetic material that becomes temporarily magnetised inside a magnetic field
Materials that can be strongly magnetised: iron, steel, cobalt, nickel
Easy to magnetise and demagnetise — iron is the classic example

keyPoints.memTip: Iron is SOFT — easy in, easy out. Steel is HARD — once it's in, it stays.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Many students confuse 'magnetic' with 'ferromagnetic' — emphasise that only iron, cobalt, nickel, and steel are ferromagnetic. Aluminium is often wrongly thought to be magnetic because it is shiny and metal-coloured.

keyPoints.heading: Magnetic field lines
keyPoints.content: A magnetic field is a region of space where a force acts on a magnetic material (or current-carrying wire). We represent magnetic fields using field lines (also called flux lines). The direction of a field line shows the direction a free north pole would move — which means outside the magnet, field lines go from north to south; inside the magnet, they go from south to north (field lines are always continuous closed loops). Key rules for field lines: • Field lines never cross — if they did, that point would have two field directions, which is impossible. • Where field lines are closer together, the field is stronger. Where they spread apart, the field is weaker. The field is strongest near the poles. • Arrows on field lines always point from N to S (outside the magnet). To plot field lines, you use a small plotting compass. Place it near the magnet — the compass needle aligns with the local field direction. Move the compass along, marking each position, to trace out the field lines. Earth's magnetic field: Earth acts like a giant bar magnet — its field lines emerge from near the geographic South Pole and enter near the geographic North Pole. This means Earth's magnetic south pole is physically located near its geographic north pole. A compass needle's north pole is attracted to Earth's magnetic south — so compass needles point toward geographic north. This has been used for navigation for thousands of years.
writeBullets: Magnetic field — region where a force acts on a magnetic material
Field lines run from N to S outside the magnet (and S to N inside)
Closer field lines = stronger field; further apart = weaker field
Field lines never cross each other
Plot field lines using a small compass — needle aligns with local field
Earth's magnetic south pole is near its geographic north pole — that's why compasses point north

keyTerms.def: A region where a force acts on a magnetic material or current-carrying wire
Lines showing the direction and strength of a magnetic field; run N to S outside the magnet
A measure of the strength of a magnetic field — stronger where field lines are closer

keyPoints.memTip: Field lines: Never Cross, North to South (outside), Closer = Stronger.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often draw field lines crossing — stress that this is always wrong. Also clarify the Earth's poles: Earth's geographic north = Earth's magnetic south, which is why a compass north pole is attracted to it.

keyPoints.heading: Electromagnetism — magnetic fields from currents
keyPoints.content: In 1820, Hans Ørsted discovered that a current-carrying wire produces a magnetic field around it. This was revolutionary — it linked electricity and magnetism for the first time. The field around a straight wire forms concentric circles around the wire. The direction of the field follows the right-hand grip rule: wrap your right hand around the wire with your thumb pointing in the direction of conventional current (from + to −); your fingers curl in the direction of the magnetic field. Reverse the current → the field reverses direction. Factors that affect the strength of the field around a wire: • Current — more current → stronger magnetic field • Distance from the wire — further away → weaker field A solenoid is a coil of wire. When current flows through it, the field inside the solenoid is uniform and strong — and the overall field pattern is identical to a bar magnet's, with a clear north pole and south pole at either end. The right-hand grip rule still applies: grip the solenoid with the right hand, fingers curling in the direction of current around the coil — thumb points to the north pole. Adding a soft iron core inside the solenoid dramatically increases the strength of the magnetic field, because iron concentrates the field lines. This is the basis of an electromagnet.
writeBullets: Current-carrying wire produces a circular magnetic field around it
Right-hand grip rule — thumb = current direction, fingers = field direction
More current or closer to wire → stronger field
Solenoid (coil of wire) produces a field like a bar magnet — with N and S poles
Iron core inside a solenoid dramatically increases field strength
Reversing current direction reverses the field direction

keyTerms.def: A coil of wire that produces a bar-magnet-like field when current flows through it
Thumb points in direction of current; fingers curl in direction of the magnetic field
A solenoid with an iron core — produces a strong magnetic field that can be switched on and off

keyPoints.memTip: Solenoid = bar magnet shape. Add iron core = electromagnet. Right hand: thumb = current, fingers = field.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Ensure students can apply the right-hand grip rule to both a straight wire (fingers curl around the wire) and a solenoid (fingers curl in the direction the current flows around the coil, thumb points to N pole).

keyPoints.heading: Electromagnets and their uses
keyPoints.content: An electromagnet is a solenoid with a soft iron core. It is far stronger than a solenoid without a core because the iron concentrates and amplifies the magnetic field. Crucially, it can be switched on and off by controlling the current — this makes it enormously useful compared to permanent magnets. Factors that affect the strength of an electromagnet: • Number of coil turns — more turns → stronger field • Current — larger current → stronger field • Iron core — much stronger than an air-core solenoid Key uses of electromagnets: • Electric bells: current activates electromagnet → attracts iron striker → hits bell → breaks circuit → electromagnet off → spring returns striker → circuit closes again → repeats • Relays: electronically controlled switches — a small current in a control circuit activates an electromagnet that closes a switch in a separate high-current circuit. Used where it is dangerous to switch large currents manually. • Circuit breakers: a fault current creates a large magnetic force that trips a lever, breaking the circuit. Resettable (unlike fuses). • Loudspeakers: interaction between a permanent magnet and an electromagnet (voice coil) moves a cone back and forth, producing sound waves. • MRI scanners: extremely powerful superconducting electromagnets (cooled to near absolute zero) create fields strong enough to align hydrogen atoms in the body. • Scrapyard cranes: large electromagnets lift and release steel and iron scrap — impossible with permanent magnets.
writeBullets: Electromagnet = solenoid with soft iron core — switchable, much stronger than air core
Strength increased by: more turns, larger current, iron core
Relays — small current switches a large current circuit safely
Circuit breakers — fault current trips a magnetic lever, breaking the circuit
Loudspeakers — voice coil and permanent magnet interact to vibrate a cone
MRI scanners — superconducting electromagnets align hydrogen atoms in the body

keyTerms.def: A solenoid with an iron core — switchable magnetic field controlled by current
A switch controlled by an electromagnet — small current controls a large current circuit
Zero electrical resistance at very low temperatures — used for very powerful electromagnets in MRI

keyPoints.memTip: Electromagnets: Switch it on, switch it off. More turns + more current + iron core = stronger.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often forget that relays serve a safety purpose — the control circuit and the high-current circuit are electrically isolated. This is important in exam questions about car starter motors and similar contexts.

tables.title: Permanent magnets vs induced magnets vs electromagnets
rows: Placed in an external magnetic field
Current through a solenoid with iron core
Adjustable — varies with current and turns
Iron nail in a magnetic field

0.question: A student places a steel paperclip near a bar magnet. The paperclip is attracted to the magnet. When the paperclip is removed from the field, it remains slightly magnetic. Which statement best explains this?
options: Steel is magnetically hard — it retains magnetism after being removed from a field
Steel is magnetically soft — it loses magnetism easily when removed from a field
The paperclip became a permanent magnet because steel cannot be demagnetised
The paperclip was already magnetic before being placed near the bar magnet
0.correctFeedback: Correct. Steel is magnetically hard — it is harder to magnetise than iron, but once magnetised it retains its magnetism. This is why steel is used for permanent magnets.
0.wrongFeedback: Think about what 'magnetically hard' and 'magnetically soft' mean. Iron is soft (easy to magnetise, easy to demagnetise). Steel is hard — it holds onto its magnetism.

2.question: A current-carrying wire is wound into a solenoid. What happens to the magnetic field strength if the number of turns is doubled and the current is halved?
options: The field strength stays roughly the same
2.correctFeedback: Correct. Doubling the turns doubles the field effect; halving the current halves it. These two changes cancel out, so the overall field strength stays roughly the same.
2.wrongFeedback: Both turns and current affect field strength. Doubling turns doubles the field; halving the current halves it. Work out the net effect: 2 × 0.5 = 1 — back to the original strength.

3.question: Which of the following is the best reason for using an electromagnet instead of a permanent magnet in a scrapyard crane?
options: The electromagnet can be switched off to release the metal scrap
The electromagnet is cheaper to produce than a permanent magnet
The electromagnet produces a stronger field in all directions
Permanent magnets do not attract iron and steel
3.correctFeedback: Exactly right. The key advantage is that the electromagnet can be switched off by cutting the current — this drops the metal scrap precisely where needed. A permanent magnet could not do this.
3.wrongFeedback: Think about what makes electromagnets uniquely useful compared to permanent magnets. The clue is in what happens when you switch off the current.

commonMistakes: Drawing field lines that cross each other — field lines can never cross; each point in a field has only one direction.
Thinking aluminium is magnetic because it is a shiny metal — only ferromagnetic materials (iron, steel, cobalt, nickel) are attracted to magnets.
Confusing magnetically soft and hard — iron is SOFT (easy magnetise/demagnetise), steel is HARD (retains magnetism). Soft iron is used for cores; steel for permanent magnets.
Saying field lines go from S to N outside the magnet — field lines always go from N to S outside the magnet (that is the direction a north pole would move).
Thinking a DC current can work in a transformer — transformers only work with AC. A steady DC creates a constant magnetic field — no change means no induced EMF.
examTips: When describing a magnetic field, always state the direction of field lines (N to S outside) and that closer lines mean a stronger field.
For electromagnet questions, three factors increase strength: more turns, larger current, iron core. Mention all three if asked.
The right-hand grip rule: thumb = direction of conventional current; fingers curl in the direction of the magnetic field around the wire.
revisionCardBullets: Permanent magnet — always magnetic; N and S poles; unlike poles attract, like poles repel
Induced magnet — magnetised by a field; loses magnetism when removed
Iron = magnetically soft (easy to magnetise/demagnetise); steel = hard (retains magnetism)
Ferromagnetic materials: iron, steel, cobalt, nickel only
Magnetic field lines: N to S outside magnet; never cross; closer = stronger field
Right-hand grip rule: thumb = current direction; fingers = field direction around wire
Solenoid = coil of wire; field like a bar magnet with N and S poles
Electromagnet = solenoid + iron core; switchable; stronger with more turns, more current
Relay: small current controls large current — electromagnet closes a switch
Earth's geographic north = Earth's magnetic south (compass N pole attracted to it)
Plot field lines using a plotting compass — needle aligns with field direction
Non-magnetic materials (aluminium, copper, plastic) are NOT attracted to magnets
